content:
2D conductive bimetallic metal-organic framework membranes for ultrafast degradation of organic pollutants Jian Hu , Jue Hou , Chen Zhao , Yuyu Su , Huacheng Zhang * Chemical and Environmental Engineering, School of Engineering, RMIT University, Melbourne, VIC, 3000, Australia

A R T I C L E I N F O Keywords:2D metal-organic frameworksBimetallic conductive membranesFenton-like processHydroxyl radicalsSulfate radicals

citation.title: 2D conductive bimetallic metal-organic framework membranes for ultrafast degradation of organic pollutants

content: A B S T R A C T Peroxymonosulfate (PMS)-based advanced oxidation processes are commonly recognized as an effective strategy for eliminating refractory organic pollutants from water. While significant efforts have been made to develop PMS activation efficiency, the low conductivity of PMS activators and the short lifespan of the reactive species still limit their catalytic performance. In this study, we synthesize a series of bimetallic conductive metal-organic framework (cMOFs) membranes, ZnCo-Tetrakis(4-carboxyphenyl)porphyrin (ZnCo-TCPP), by precisely tuning the morphology and conductivity of two-dimensional frameworks through controlled Zn and Co ion ratios. The resulting membrane obtained 100 % removal efficiency of bisphenol A and five other pollutants, demonstrating a high-water flux of 308 L m-2 h-1 bar-1 and an exceptionally fast reaction rate constant of 2000 min-1. Quenching tests and chemical probe analysis confirmed that hydroxyl radicals (⋅OH) and sulfate radicals (SO4-) played dominant roles in the ZnCo-TCPP/PMS system. This study presents a novel strategy for morphology engineering in cMOFs and advances their application in high-performance PMS-based water purification.

citation.title: 2D conductive bimetallic metal-organic framework membranes for ultrafast degradation of organic pollutants

content: 1. Introduction Peroxymonosulfate-based advanced oxidation processes (PMSAOPs) have been merged as highly effective strategies for treating nonbiodegradable organic contaminants in water. These processes generate highly reactive oxygen species (ROSs) with strong redox potential and high oxidizing capacity by breaking the O–O and O–H bonds in PMS molecules, enabling efficient degradation of a wide range of contami­nants in wastewater [1–3]. The production of radicals has been sub­stantially enhanced when PMS is activated through various approaches, such as light, electricity, heat, transition metal ions or carbon materials [4,5]. Despite these advances, low ROS yield and limited electron transport in conventional PMS activators still restrict overall performance. Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) have garnered considerable attention for catalytic application due to their high surface area, struc­tural tunability, and chemical versatility [6–9]. MOFs-based PMS acti­vators, such as ZIF-8 [10], MIL-53 [11], MIL-101 [12], UiO-66 [13], and MOF-74 [14], have shown promise; however, their inherently low electrical conductivity remains a major bottleneck. To improve con­ductivity, researchers have explored strategies, such as carbonizing MOFs at high temperatures [15], and integrating with conductive materials like graphene oxide [16], carbon nitride [17], and MXenes [18].

citation.title: 2D conductive bimetallic metal-organic framework membranes for ultrafast degradation of organic pollutants

content: carbonizing MOFs at high temperatures [15], and integrating with conductive materials like graphene oxide [16], carbon nitride [17], and MXenes [18]. For example, atomically dispersed cobalt on nitrogen-co-doped carbon (Co-SA) was synthesized by carbonizing the methylimidazole-based MOFs, leading to almost 100 % PMS conversion to active species [19]. Similarly, FexMn6-xCo4-N@C was obtained by calcining MnyFe1-y[Co(CN)6]0.67⋅nH2O in a nitrogen atmosphere, which dramatically enhanced the BPA removal efficiency by increasing the content of Mn4N [20]. However, these post-treatment methods often compromise the intrinsic microstructure of MOFs, making the process energy-intensive, costly, and potentially polluting. Developing pristine conductive MOFs (cMOFs) with high electrical conductivity provides a promising route to maximize their intrinsic structural and electronic properties. Among them, two-dimensional (2D) cMOFs offer unique advances for confined catalysis in membrane-based systems. For instance, the conductive 2D phthalocyanine-based MOF (NiPc-NiO4), with an impressive conductivity of 4.8 * 10-5 S m-1, was achieved by integrating Ni-phthalocyanine motif (NiPc) into a porous, intrinsically conductive nickel-catecholate-linked 2D nanosheets [21]. This material obtained 100 % electro-reduction efficiency of CO2 to CO.

citation.title: 2D conductive bimetallic metal-organic framework membranes for ultrafast degradation of organic pollutants

content: porous, intrinsically conductive nickel-catecholate-linked 2D nanosheets [21]. This material obtained 100 % electro-reduction efficiency of CO2 to CO. Cu3(HOTP)2 [22] and Fe(1,2,3-triazolate)2(BF4)0.33 [23] were synthe­sized with the conductivities of 2.9 * 10-3 and 3 * 10-3 S m-1, respectively, demonstrating applications potentials in electronics

citation.title: 2D conductive bimetallic metal-organic framework membranes for ultrafast degradation of organic pollutants

content: Available online 14 July 2025 0376-7388/ 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. This is an open access article under the CC BY license ( http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/ ). fabrication, and electrochemical processes, respectively. While sub­stantial progress has been made in tuning intralayer conductivity via metal–ligand modification, this approach often requires significant structural changes, limiting precise control over electronic properties. In contrast, interlayer engineering within 2D frameworks offers a more refined strategy to tune the electronic band structure, dielectric prop­erties, and charge transport in cMOFs. Two-dimensional conductive metal-organic frameworks (2D cMOFs) feature layer-stacked structures with in-plane π-extended conjugation and out-of-plane π-orbital overlap. This architecture facilitates efficient charge carrier transport both within the 2D plane and along the π-stacking direction. As a result, 2D c-MOFs exhibit significantly enhanced electrical conductivity compared to conventional MOFs. Yang et al. [24] precisely tune interlayer spacing of Ni3(HATI_CX)2 from 3.40 to 3.70 Å, leading to a widened band gap, suppressed carrier mobilities, and significant improvement of the See­beck coefficient. Despite its potential, interlayer engineering within 2D frameworks remains underexplored, particularly in the context of PMS activation and environmental remediation.

citation.title: 2D conductive bimetallic metal-organic framework membranes for ultrafast degradation of organic pollutants

content: ntial, interlayer engineering within 2D frameworks remains underexplored, particularly in the context of PMS activation and environmental remediation. This work presents a strategy for interlayer engineering in 2D cMOFs, enabling precise control over conductivity for pollutant removal via PMS activation. Metal ions with distinct radii were utilized to pre­cisely modulate the interlayer spacing of bimetallic MOFs ZnCo-TCPP [TCPP refers to Tetrakis(4-carboxyphenyl)porphyrin], which featured ultrathin 2D nanosheets with a thickness of less than 10 nm. A mem­brane reactor, formed by filtering ZnCo-TCPP nanosheets onto a poly­ethersulfone (PES) support, served as a nanofluidic platform for PMS activation in pollutant removal. Bisphenol A (BPA), a widely used in­dustrial additive commonly found in wastewater and biological systems, was selected as the target pollutant for this study. Typically, the ZnCoTCPP membrane achieved complete removal of BPA and five other pollutants, demonstrating a reaction rate constant of around 2000 min-1, which was six orders of magnitude higher than that of bulk ZnCoTCPP nanosheets. Additionally, the nanofluidic ZnCo-TCPP membrane surpassed previously reported membrane-based AOP systems by 5to 500-fold (Table S1). This work paves the way for developing cMOFsbased PMS activation materials while providing valuable insights into the structure-function relationship of MOFs. 2. Experimental methods 2.1.

citation.title: 2D conductive bimetallic metal-organic framework membranes for ultrafast degradation of organic pollutants

content: g cMOFsbased PMS activation materials while providing valuable insights into the structure-function relationship of MOFs. 2. Experimental methods 2.1. Materials Zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn(NO3)2⋅6H2O), cobalt (II) nitrate hexa­hydrate (Co(NO3)2⋅6H2O), polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP, average mol wt 40,000), pyrazine (99 %), N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF, 99.8 %), bisphenol A, potassium peroxymonosulfate (PMS, KHSO5⋅0.5KH­SO4⋅0.5K2SO4), sodium sulfate, tetracycline, phenol, methylene blue, methyl orange, Congo red, potassium sulfate (K2SO4), p-benzoquinone (BQ), tert-Butyl alcohol (TBA), L-histidine, nitro blue tetrazolium (NBT), terephthalic acid, benzoic acid were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Pty. Ltd. Methanol was purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific, Australia. Deionized water was sourced from the Milli-Q System. All materials were used as received without any further purification. 2.2. Synthesis of ZnCo-TCPP nanosheets and membrane Synthesis of 100Zn-TCPP nanosheets. Zn(NO3)2⋅6H2O (4.5 mg, 0.015 mmol), pyrazine (0.8 mg, 0.01 mmol), and PVP (20.0 mg) were dissolved in 12 mL of a DMF-ethanol mixture (3:1, v/v) within a 20 mL capped vial. Subsequently, TCPP (4.0 mg, 0.005 mmol) dissolved in 4 mL of a DMF-ethanol mixture (3:1, v/v) was gradually added dropwise under continuous stirring. The mixture was sonicated for 10 min, then heated to 80 ◦C and maintained at that temperature for 16 h to complete the reaction.

citation.title: 2D conductive bimetallic metal-organic framework membranes for ultrafast degradation of organic pollutants

content: continuous stirring. The mixture was sonicated for 10 min, then heated to 80 ◦C and maintained at that temperature for 16 h to complete the reaction. The obtained purple sediment was washed twice with ethanol and separated by centrifugation at 8000 rpm. for 10 min. Finally, the Zn-TCPP nanosheets were resuspended in 10 mL of ethanol.

citation.title: 2D conductive bimetallic metal-organic framework membranes for ultrafast degradation of organic pollutants

content: Synthesis of ZnCo-TCPP nanosheets with varying Zn/Co ratios. The synthesis process remained consistent, except for the varying Zn (NO3)2⋅6H2O/Co(NO3)2⋅6H2O ratios. For instance, in the preparation of Zn40Co60-TCPP, 0.009 mmol Co(NO3)2⋅6H2O and 0.006 mmol Zn (NO3)2⋅6H2O were combined in the precursor. The subsequent steps followed the same procedure as for Zn-TCPP nanosheets. Synthesis of ZnCo-TCPP membrane. The ZnCo-TCPP membranes were fabricated by a self-assembly method under vacuum-assisted filtration. Firstly, varying amounts (2 –9 mg) of ZnCo-TCPP nano­sheets were dispersed in 50 ml ethanol and ultrasonicated for 10 min. Then the ZnCo-TCPP suspension was vacuum-filtered through a porous polyethersulfone (PES) support membrane. This membrane, featuring a mean pore size of 0.03 mm and a 13 mm diameter, was supplied by Sterlitech Corporation (USA). 2.3. Materials characterizations The morphology and elemental distribution of the nanosheets were examined using transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL JEMF200, Japan) operating at 200 kV and scanning electron microscopy (SEM, FEI Verios 460L SEM). The crystalline structure of the fabricated materials was characterized using powder X-ray diffraction (XRD, D4 Endeavour, Bruker) with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å). X-ray photo­electron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis (Thermo Scientific K-Alpha XPS) was employed to investigate the elemental compositions and valence states of the constituent elements using an Al Kα radiation source.

citation.title: 2D conductive bimetallic metal-organic framework membranes for ultrafast degradation of organic pollutants

content: ic K-Alpha XPS) was employed to investigate the elemental compositions and valence states of the constituent elements using an Al Kα radiation source. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, FT-IR/FIR Spectrometer Frontier, PerkinElmer) was employed to analyze the surface properties of the samples. 2.4. Catalytic performance of ZnCo-TCPP membrane All degradation experiments were conducted at 20 ◦C under standard atmospheric pressure. In a standard experiment, 100 mL of a 20 mg L-1 BPA solution was passed through the Zn/Co-TCPP membrane to estab­lish adsorption-desorption equilibrium. The solution with 5 ppm BPA and 200 ppm PMS was passed through the ZnCo-TCPP membrane under a working pressure of 0.08 MPa. Permeate samples were taken at 20-min intervals and analyzed via high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC, Agilent 1220 Infinity LC) at a detection wavelength of 230 nm. A Poroshell 120 EC-C18 column (4.6 * 150 mm, 2.7 μm) was used for separation, with a methanol/water (70:30, v/v) mobile phase. The BPA degradation efficiency was determined using the following equation: BPA degradation efficiency (%) =(1 -CP

where CP and CF represent the concentrations of BPA in the permeate and feed solutions, respectively. The flux of the ZIF-67/GO-x membrane was determined using the following equation: J =VS * T(2) where J represents the permeate flux (L m-2 h-1), V is the permeate volume (L), S stands for the effective membrane surface area (m2), and T is the experimental duration (h). For the batch suspension reaction, an equal amount of Zn/Co-TCPP nanosheets was introduced into 50 mL of BPA (20 ppm) buffer solu­tion. After stirring for 30 min to reach adsorption-desorption equilib­rium, PMS (200 ppm) was added to the mixture to initiate the reaction. Samples were collected every 20 min and analyzed using HPLC. The concentration of leached cobalt ions was measured using an inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometer (ICP, 7700 Series ICP-MS, Agilent).

citation.title: 2D conductive bimetallic metal-organic framework membranes for ultrafast degradation of organic pollutants

content: 2.5. Detection of active species Active radicals, such as hydroxyl radicals (⋅OH) and sulfate radicals (SO4-), were detected using fluorescence spectroscopy with terephthalic acid [25] and benzoic acid [26] solutions as probes, respectively, employing a FluoroMax-4 Spectrofluorometer (Horiba). Superoxide radicals (O2⋅-) were detected using nitro blue tetrazolium (NBT) [27] solution as a probe with UV–Vis spectrophotometry (Series UV–Vis–NIR Spectrophotometer, Agilent). Electrochemical characterization, including current–time curve re­sponses and Nyquist plots at open circuit potential, was performed using an electrochemical workstation (Vertex, Ivium Technologies). A satu­rated Ag/AgCl electrode and a platinum rod were used as the reference and counter electrodes, respectively. The working electrode was made from fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) glass (1 * 2 cm, OPV Tech Co., Ltd, China), onto which ZnCo-TCPP nanosheets (100 mL, 100 mg L-1) were coated three times. After each coating, the electrode was dried in an oven at 80 ◦C for 10 min. A 0.2 M Na2SO4 solution was used as the electrolyte.

citation.title: 2D conductive bimetallic metal-organic framework membranes for ultrafast degradation of organic pollutants

content: 3. Results and discussion 3.1. Microanalysis and characterizations Bimetallic cMOFs of ZnCo-TCPP with tailored Zn/Co ion ratios were synthesized via a surfactant-assisted bottom-up method. Typically, the TCPP ligand coordinates with metal ions to form TCPP bridged Zn2(COO)4 paddlewheel clusters. The use of polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) as a surfactant selectively regulates MOF growth by binding to specific crystal facets, promoting anisotropic growth and resulting in ultrathin 2D square-grid nanosheets. In an AB stacking configuration, the zinc atoms located at the centres of the porphyrin rings are aligned with the zinc atoms in the paddlewheel metal nodes, resulting in a 2D MOFs structure (Fig. 1a). Fig. S1 shows the scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of Zn/Co-TCPP products with Zn/Co molar ratios from 0 % to 100 %. The 2D nanosheet morphology can be achieved when the zinc proportion exceeds 40 %. Fig. 1b shows an SEM image of a Zn40Co60-TCPP nanosheet of size in micrometers. TEM image of the nanosheet was shown in Fig. 1c and d. High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) imaging was employed to investigate the crystal structure of ZnCo-TCPP

content: Fig. 1. (a) Schematic illustration of the synthesis process of ZnCo-TCPP nanosheets, (b) SEM, (c),(d)TEM images, inset: SAED pattern, (e) HRTEM images of Zn40Co60-TCPP. (f)–(h), AFM images and the corresponding thickness of Zn40Co60-TCPP. (i)–(n) STEM micrograph and the corresponding EDS mappings of Zn40Co60-TCPP. J. Hu et al. Journal of Membrane Science 734 (2025) 124447 nanosheets, as shown in Fig. 1e. The lattice fringe observed with an interplanar distance of 1.64 nm, corresponds to the (100) plane of the Zn-TCPP crystal and closely matches the 1.67 nm value derived from the X-ray structure model [28]. This alignment highlights the high crystal­linity of the synthesized cMOFs. Additionally, the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns, shown in the inset of Fig. 1d, further confirm the interior crystal structure. Moreover, the XRD patterns were analyzed to verify the high crystal structure of ZnCo-TCPP nanosheets. The AFM image reveals that the thickness of Zn40Co60-TCPP nanosheets is approximately 7–8 nm (Fig. 1f–h). EDS mapping anal­ysis (Fig. 1i–n) showed a uniform distribution of Zn and Co atoms within the conductive MOFs, confirming both the successful metal substitution and the synthesis of bimetallic cMOFs. Moreover, the EDS mapping re­veals an approximate Zn/Co atomic ratio of ~0.70, which aligns well with the initial precursor composition, as corroborated by ICP-MS analysis. The SEM image of the Zn40Co60-TCPP membrane (Fig.

citation.title: 2D conductive bimetallic metal-organic framework membranes for ultrafast degradation of organic pollutants

content: ~0.70, which aligns well with the initial precursor composition, as corroborated by ICP-MS analysis. The SEM image of the Zn40Co60-TCPP membrane (Fig. S3a) reveals that the surface is uniformly smooth, exhibiting no visible pinholes or cracks. The cross-sectional view (Fig. S3b) of the Zn40Co60-TCPP membrane displays a well-defined laminar structure, effectively retaining active surface and edges relative to the original 2D configu­ration. Additionally, the 2D nanosheets generated a confined nano­structure, creating numerous nanochannels within the interlayer spaces. However, as the zinc proportion decreases, the lateral sizes of the nanosheets become smaller. For instance, Co-TCPP even exhibits a nanoplate morphology with a lateral size ranging from 200 to 400 nm. This phenomenon is attributed to the inert nature of Zn2+ ions, which slow down the reaction rate, enabling the formation of larger nano­sheets. ZnCo-TCPP was synthesized using a binary mixture of metal precursors in varying proportions. Due to the nearly identical structures of precursors, the final Zn/Co ions ratios were analyzed using ICP-MS techniques (Table S2). This analysis consistently demonstrated that the final metal compositions closely matched those of the precursors. As shown in Fig. 2a, the peaks indicate the tetragonal structure of ZnCo-TCPP MOFs.

citation.title: 2D conductive bimetallic metal-organic framework membranes for ultrafast degradation of organic pollutants

content: final metal compositions closely matched those of the precursors. As shown in Fig. 2a, the peaks indicate the tetragonal structure of ZnCo-TCPP MOFs. Due to the tendency of ZnCo-TCPP nanosheets to lie flat on a solid substrate with a (001) preferred orientation, most crystal diffraction peaks are undetected. Instead, only a broad peak at approx­imately 18◦, attributed to the (004) plane, is observed. Similar XRD tendencies can be also obtained in other 2D nanosheets [29–31]. Charge transport in cMOFs typically occurs through two modes: interlayer transport and intralayer transport [32]. The chemical activity and ionic radius of the metal centres in cMOFs influence the extent of intralayer π-d and interlayer π-π conjugations. Altering single-metal centres can lead to significant and unpredictable changes in these charge transport mechanisms. In contrast, incorporating bimetallic or polymetallic centres allows for more controlled tuning of interlayer transport, enabling the design of precisely adjusted cMOF structures for electronic applications. Moreover, powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) analysis (Fig. 2a) revealed that ZnCo-TCPP shares an isostructural crystal structure with both Zn-TCPP and Co-TCPP. The peak near 18◦, associated with the (001) reflection, implied an ordered stacking with an interlayer distance of approximately 4.8 Å. As the Zn content increases, the diffraction peak shifts to lower angles (Fig.

citation.title: 2D conductive bimetallic metal-organic framework membranes for ultrafast degradation of organic pollutants

content: an ordered stacking with an interlayer distance of approximately 4.8 Å. As the Zn content increases, the diffraction peak shifts to lower angles (Fig. 2a and Table S3), sug­gesting a widening of the interlayer spacing. This shift is attributed to the larger ionic radius of Zn2+ (RCo2+ < RZn2+) [33], which leads to a reduction in the orbital overlap between neighbouring O and C atoms. Therefore, Zn2+ doping in Co-TCPP effectively increases the interlayer spacing and weakens the ligand orbital overlap. As a result, both charge transport and bandgaps can be precisely adjusted by varying the inter­layer spacing along the c-axis. The Nyquist plots (Fig. 2b) confirm that Zn40Co60-TCPP exhibits the smallest semicircle diameter among all the 2D morphology ZnCo-TCPP

citation.title: 2D conductive bimetallic metal-organic framework membranes for ultrafast degradation of organic pollutants

content: Fig. 2. (a) The PXRD patterns corresponding to (004) reflection of ZnCo-TCPP. (b) Nyquist plots at open circuit potential for different reaction systems. (c) O 1s spectra, (d) Co 2p spectra, and (e) Zn 2p spectra of ZnCo-TCPP. J. Hu et al. Journal of Membrane Science 734 (2025) 124447 materials. As the zinc proportion increases, the semicircle diameter becomes larger, indicating that the electronic conductivities of ZnCoTCPP correlate with the interlayer spacing. In other words, the elec­tronic conductivities increase with decreasing interlayer spacing. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was applied to explore the coordination structures in the cMOFs. As shown in Fig. S4a, the disappearance of the N–H vibration peak at 3070 and 3320 cm-1 [34,35] suggests successful coordination between nitrogen and metal ions on the substituted pyrrole ring, providing strong evidence for the successful formation of ZnCo-TCPP. Fig. S4b demonstrated that a red shift in the C––O stretching vibration of PVP was observed, shifting from 1660 cm-1 to 1630 and 1624 cm-1 after mixing with Co(NO3)2 and Zn (NO3)2, confirming the stronger interactions between the C––O groups in PVP and metal ions [36,37]. This interaction suggests that PVP ad­heres to the nanosheet surface after nucleation, promoting anisotropic growth and ultimately resulting in thinner ZnCo-TCPP nanosheets.

citation.title: 2D conductive bimetallic metal-organic framework membranes for ultrafast degradation of organic pollutants

content: XPS analysis was conducted to determine the chemical states present in ZnCo-TCPP. The survey spectra (Fig. S5) of Zn-TCPP, Co60Zn40TCPP, and Co-TCPP revealed the presence of C, N, O, Zn, and Co ele­ments. Fig. 2c–e presents the O 1s, Co 2p and Zn 2p spectra of the bimetallic cMOFs. The O 1s spectrum (Fig. 2c) of ZnCo-TCPP displayed three peaks at 531.9, 533.4, and 537.1 eV, corresponding to C–O, C––O, and π-π* bonding configurations, respectively, which verify the π-conjugation facilitated by non-covalent interactions [38]. The peaks of Co 2p3/2 (II) in Co-TCPP and Zn40Co60-TCPP are both observed at 782.38 eV (Fig. 2d). In comparison to the Co 2p3/2 peak in Co3O4 [39], a more significant shift towards higher binding energy regions appears in ZnCo-TCPP, suggesting a lower electron density around the Co atoms. A similar effect was observed in the Zn 2p spectra as well (Fig. 2e) [40]. These results suggest that the variations in the electronic structure of the cMOFs are attributed to interlayer charge transport within the 2D frameworks.

citation.title: 2D conductive bimetallic metal-organic framework membranes for ultrafast degradation of organic pollutants

(d) Per­formance comparison between the Zn40Co60-TCPP/PMS system and previously developed catalytic membranes. (e) Stability assessment and Co2+/Zn2+ leaching behavior of the Zn40Co60-TCPP/PMS system over time. Reaction conditions: [BPA] = 20 mg L-1, [PMS] = 200 ppm, pH = 6, and T = 293 K. J. Hu et al. citation.title: 2D conductive bimetallic metal-organic framework membranes for ultrafast degradation of organic pollutants

content: 3.2. Catalytic performance and stability The catalytic performance of ZnCo-TCPP membranes for PMS acti­vation was assessed by examining the BPA removal efficiency (Fig. 3a). As shown in Fig. 3b, PMS alone shows minimal degradation efficiency, with only ~1 % of BPA removed within 100 min, indicating that the selfdecomposition of PMS to produce ROS was negligible without a catalyst. Similarly, in the absence of PMS, the physical adsorption of Zn40Co60TCPP membrane resulted in just 6.2 % BPA removal, suggesting that adsorption contributed only marginally to BPA degradation. Further­more, it is evident that Zn40Co60-TCPP and Co-TCPP demonstrate notable differences in BPA removal efficiency, highlighting the critical role of optimal Co-doping in significantly reducing the interlayer spacing. The impact of cobalt doping on catalytic performance was further assessed. As shown in Fig. S6, the BPA removal efficiencies for cobalt and zinc ions at equal concentrations in the precursors of ZnCo-TCPP cMOFs were compared. Specifically, 0.3 %, 6.5 %, and 11.5 % of BPA were removed by 0.015 mM Zn2+, 0.006 mM Zn2++ 0.009 mM Co2+, and 0.015 mM Co2+, respectively. In contrast, Zn-TCPP, Zn40Co60TCPP, and Co-TCPP achieved BPA removal efficiencies of 10.4 %, 100 %, and 86.1 %, respectively. These results suggest that the presence of Co2+ alone contributes modestly to catalytic performance.

citation.title: 2D conductive bimetallic metal-organic framework membranes for ultrafast degradation of organic pollutants

content: ncies of 10.4 %, 100 %, and 86.1 %, respectively. These results suggest that the presence of Co2+ alone contributes modestly to catalytic performance. Instead, the enhanced activity of ZnCo-TCPP is primarily attributed to the optimized interlayer spacing and associated improvements in electronic conduc­tivity, rather than the redox activity of Co2+ alone. Zn40Co60-TCPP exhibited the highest performance among all com­positions, achieving 100 % BPA removal. As Co content increased up to 60 %, interlayer spacing decreased, thereby enhancing charge transport and PMS activation. However, further increases in Co content (e.g., CoTCPP) resulted in diminished performance. This decline is likely due to a significant reduction in nanosheet size (as seen in Fig. 1 and Fig. S1–S2), which reduces the number of accessible active sites and weakens membrane integrity. Membranes with high Co content (≥60 %) also exhibited poor mechanical stability, frequently peeling off from the PES support. These findings underscore the importance of balancing inter­layer spacing and nanosheet morphology for optimal performance. The above observations indicate that the morphology has a greater impact on catalytic efficiency than microscopic interlayer spacing. Although membranes with a high cobalt concentration (≥60 %) exhibit excellent BPA removal efficiencies, they tend to peel off from the PES support, indicating the need for further improvements in membrane stability.

citation.title: 2D conductive bimetallic metal-organic framework membranes for ultrafast degradation of organic pollutants

content: it excellent BPA removal efficiencies, they tend to peel off from the PES support, indicating the need for further improvements in membrane stability. Accordingly, this study emphasizes fine-tuning the interlayer spacing in 2D nanosheets with cobalt ratios below 60 %. Additionally, compared to bulk Zn40Co60-TCPP nanosheets (suspension-based sys­tem), the nanofluidic membrane configuration exhibited vastly superior catalytic activity (Fig. 3b). BPA was completely degraded within just 57.3 ms in the membrane system—surpassing the performance of co¯ınventional AOPs. The corresponding first-order rate constant for BPA removal reached 2000 min-1 (0.0338 ms-1, inset of Fig. 3c), approxi­mately six orders of magnitude greater than that of non-confined het­erogeneous Zn40Co60-TCPP nanosheets (0.002 min-1, Fig. S7). This value also exceeded those of other membrane-based catalytic systems by up to seven orders of magnitude (0.002–100 min-1, Fig. 3d and Table S1). The catalytic performance of Zn40Co60-TCPP was chosen as the following studies and herein was systematically evaluated through BPA degradation studies. To achieve an optimal balance between catalytic efficiency and permeance, the thickness and area of the Zn40Co60-TCPP membrane were adjusted. As shown in Fig. S8a, increasing membrane thickness enhanced catalytic performance but reduced water flux.

citation.title: 2D conductive bimetallic metal-organic framework membranes for ultrafast degradation of organic pollutants

content: f the Zn40Co60-TCPP membrane were adjusted. As shown in Fig. S8a, increasing membrane thickness enhanced catalytic performance but reduced water flux. A membrane thickness of 1.3 μm achieved complete (100 %) BPA degra­dation while maintaining a water flux of 308 L m-2 h-1 bar-1, making it the optimal choice for further experiments. Additionally, as the mem­brane diameter increased from 13 to 37 mm, both BPA removal effi­ciency and water flux remained consistent (Fig. S8a). Therefore, a 13 mm diameter was selected for subsequent studies. The stability of the Zn40Co60-TCPP membrane/PMS system was assessed through a continuous flow experiment. As shown in Fig. 3e, both the removal efficiency and flux remained stable even within 28 h. Additionally, the membrane structure after the reaction, as shown in Fig. S9a and S9b, also remained well preserved. To further evaluate its durability, metal ion leaching of the PMS activator was analyzed. The initial leaching of Zn and Co ions reached approximately 40 and 50 μg L-1, well within the World Health Organization’s (WHO) drinking water guideline of 100 mg L-1. Furthermore, the impact of the maximum leached zinc (40 μg L-1) and cobalt (50 μg L-1) concentration on BPA degradation was negligible, with homogeneous PMS activation removing less than 2 % of BPA (Fig. S9c). This confirms that BPA degradation primarily occurred through heterogeneous catalysis by the Zn40Co60-TCPP membrane rather than the minimal leaching of zinc and cobalt ions.

citation.title: 2D conductive bimetallic metal-organic framework membranes for ultrafast degradation of organic pollutants

content: degradation primarily occurred through heterogeneous catalysis by the Zn40Co60-TCPP membrane rather than the minimal leaching of zinc and cobalt ions. To assess the broader applicability of the Zn40Co60-TCPP mem­brane-based catalysis, its effectiveness in degrading five more organic pollutants was examined. As shown in Fig. S10, removal efficiencies were evaluated for various concentrations of BPA and other contami­nants in the presence of PMS. All tested pollutants at 20 ppm—including phenol, methyl orange, tetracycline, methylene blue, and congo red—were rapidly degraded, achieving 100 % removal within 48.2–74.1 ms. These findings highlight the Zn40Co60-TCPP membrane as a highly efficient platform for rapidly eliminating diverse organic contaminants from wastewater. 3.3. Active species detection and mechanism Based on the analysis above, the reactive species generated through PMS activation is crucial for BPA degradation within the Zn40Co60TCPP/PMS system. To further investigate the underlying mechanisms, quenching experiments were systematically conducted to examine the potential involvement of hydroxyl radicals (⋅OH), sulfate radicals (SO4-), superoxide radicals (O2-), and singlet oxygen (1O2) in the PMS-driven oxidation processes. TBA (kSO⋅-4 = 4–9.5 * 105 M-1 s-1, k⋅OH =3.8–7.6 * 108 M-1 s-1) and MeOH (kSO⋅-4 = 2.5 * 107 M-1 s-1, k⋅OH =9.7 * 108 M-1 s-1) served as scavengers for ⋅OH and SO4-radicals due to their high second-order reaction rates [16].

citation.title: 2D conductive bimetallic metal-organic framework membranes for ultrafast degradation of organic pollutants

content: kSO⋅-4 = 2.5 * 107 M-1 s-1, k⋅OH =9.7 * 108 M-1 s-1) served as scavengers for ⋅OH and SO4-radicals due to their high second-order reaction rates [16]. A significant inhibitory effect was observed, as BPA degradation efficiency dropped from 100 % to 55.8 % and 61.3 % in the presence of 1 M TBA and TBAOH, respec­tively (a). Superoxide radicals (O2-), a common type of reactive oxygen species (ROS) in advanced oxidation processes (AOPs), were also examined. However, the addition of 500 mM carbonate (kCO2-3 = 5 * 108 M-1 s-1) and 20 mM BQ (kBQ = 2.9 * 109 M-1 s-1) had minimal impact on BPA degradation (Fig. S11). Similarly, as shown in Fig. S12 singlet oxygen (1O2) contributed negligibly when L-His was used as a quenching agent. These findings indicate that ⋅OH and SO4- radicals played a dominant role in the Zn40Co60-TCPP membrane/PMS system. Although 1 M MeOH and TBA had a limited impact on reducing BPA removal efficiency, this may be attributed to the system’s strong oxidative potential. To further confirm the involvement of reactive species in BPA degradation, the production of ⋅OH and SO4- radicals was quantitatively evaluated using the aromatic hydroxylation of terephthalic acid [25] and benzoic acid [41], respectively. The fluorescence intensity at approximately 440 nm gradually decreased as the cobalt ratio decreased in the precursors of ZnCo-TCPP (Fig. 4b and S13a), confirming the presence and formation of ⋅OH radicals.

citation.title: 2D conductive bimetallic metal-organic framework membranes for ultrafast degradation of organic pollutants

content: ally decreased as the cobalt ratio decreased in the precursors of ZnCo-TCPP (Fig. 4b and S13a), confirming the presence and formation of ⋅OH radicals. Additionally, its trend is closely aligned with BPA degradation efficiency (Fig. 3b), further highlighting the involvement of ⋅OH radicals in the reaction. Likewise, the fluores­cence emission peak at 403 nm (Fig. 4c and S13b) confirmed the for­mation of SO4- radicals in the ZnCo-TCPP membrane/PMS systems, highlighting their involvement in BPA degradation. Superoxide radicals (O2-), another key reactive species, were also examined in this study.

citation.title: 2D conductive bimetallic metal-organic framework membranes for ultrafast degradation of organic pollutants

content: However, the UV spectra of O2- in a benzoic acid solution (Fig. S13c) showed no peaks around 259 nm [42] for any of the ZnCo-TCPP mem­brane/PMS systems, indicating that the production of O2- was negligible. These findings are consistent with the results from the previous quenching tests. It is widely recognized that the generation of active radicals is closely linked to the efficiency of electron transfer in advanced oxidation pro­cesses [43]. Therefore, electrochemical analysis was carried out to investigate the electron transfer process in the ZnCo-TCPP mem­brane/PMS systems. The current–time curves in Fig. 4d displayed a rising current response for all materials upon the introduction of PMS into the electrolyte at 200 s. However, with the increase of cobalt ratio in the ZnCo-TCPP membranes, a higher current response could be ach­ieved. Since the morphology of Co-TCPP is not 2D and the lateral size is significantly smaller than that of other materials, pollutant molecules encounter fewer active sites when passing through the MOFs channels. These findings align with the observed trends in BPA removal efficiency and active species generation. The current-time curves exhibited a similar trend to the Nyquist plots, as illustrated in Fig. 2b. This indicates that the Zn40Co60-TCPP/PMS system facilitated more efficient electron transfer between catalysts and PMS molecules, enhancing the genera­tion of active species for BPA degradation.

citation.title: 2D conductive bimetallic metal-organic framework membranes for ultrafast degradation of organic pollutants

content: tem facilitated more efficient electron transfer between catalysts and PMS molecules, enhancing the genera­tion of active species for BPA degradation. Specifically, Co2+ donated electrons to PMS, activating it to generate reactive species (SO4-). Simultaneously, the oxidation state of cobalt shifted from +2 to +3, as represented in Eq. (1). Fig. 2e presents the Zn 2p spectrum, where the initial peak at 1021.9 eV shifted slightly to

citation.title: 2D conductive bimetallic metal-organic framework membranes for ultrafast degradation of organic pollutants

content: 1021.5 eV after use. Additionally, the peak area decreased slightly after the catalytic reaction, indicating that a portion of Zn2+ on the ZnCoTCPP catalyst was converted into Zn3+ during the process, as described in Eq. (2). However, Zn3+ was not detected in the XPS spec­trum. According to the previous quantum chemical calculation [44], no thermodynamically stable Zn3+ complex exists, suggesting that Zn3+ would rapidly revert to the more stable Zn2+ state. Furthermore, the continuous redox cycling of Co3+ and Zn3+ enabled the ZnCo-TCPP catalyst to function persistently. The redox cycles occurring on its sur­face contributed to its exceptional catalytic performance. The corre­sponding reaction equations are provided in Eqs. (3) and (4). The SO5- radicals were rapidly reduced to SO4- radicals, which then reacted with water to produce ⋅OH radicals (Eqs. (5) and (6)). The ZnCo-TCPP membrane, with its large amount of exposed active sites, facilitated high radical yields and efficient utilization due to minimal mass transfer resistance. Among them, SO4- and ⋅OH radicals played a dominant role in BPA degradation (Eqs. (6) and (7)) [20,45,46]. citation.title: 2D conductive bimetallic metal-organic framework membranes for ultrafast degradation of organic pollutants

content: 2SO⋅-5 →2SO⋅-4 + O2(5) SO⋅-4 + H2O →HSO-4 + ⋅OH(6) ⋅OH/SO⋅-4 + BPA →intermediates →CO2 + H2O(7) 4. Conclusion We successfully synthesized a series of two-dimensional conductive metal–organic frameworks (ZnCo-TCPP) using a simple, energy-efficient surfactant-assisted strategy. By precisely adjusting the Zn/Co ion ratio, the interlayer spacing of the cMOFs was finely tuned, enabling control over their electronic band structure and conductivity. This interlayer engineering approach yielded nanosheets with enhanced charge trans­port and catalytic performance. The optimized Zn40Co60-TCPP mem­brane demonstrated outstanding PMS activation capability, achieving complete degradation of bisphenol A (BPA) and other pollutants with a reaction rate constant of 2000 min-1 and a high-water flux of 308 L m-2 h-1 bar-1. This performance exceeded previously reported catalytic membrane systems by up to four orders of magnitude. The enhanced activity was attributed to the synergistic effects of reduced interlayer spacing, improved electronic conductivity, and nanosheet morphology. Quenching experiments and active species detection confirmed that both sulfate radicals (SO4-) and hydroxyl radicals (⋅OH) played domi­nant roles in pollutant degradation. Electrochemical analyses further revealed that the Zn40Co60-TCPP/PMS system exhibited efficient electron transfer, supporting high radical generation and utilization ef­ficiency.

citation.title: 2D conductive bimetallic metal-organic framework membranes for ultrafast degradation of organic pollutants

content: ther revealed that the Zn40Co60-TCPP/PMS system exhibited efficient electron transfer, supporting high radical generation and utilization ef­ficiency. Overall, this study introduces a versatile and scalable strategy for interlayer engineering in cMOFs and provides valuable insights into structure–function relationships in PMS-based oxidation systems. The ZnCo-TCPP membrane platform offers significant potential for practical applications in advanced water treatment and environmental remediation.
